title: Publication of Darwin's On the Origin of Species
summary: On 24 November 1859 the London publisher John Murray issued On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life, by Charles Darwin. The first edition of 1,250 copies sold out on the day of publication. In fewer than five hundred pages Darwin proposed that the variety of life on earth had arisen not by separate acts of divine creation but by the slow accumulation of small heritable variations through descent with modification, driven by a struggle for existence. The book became the foundational text of modern biology.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The idea that species could change was not new when Charles Darwin turned to it. Jean-Baptiste Lamarck had proposed a mechanism of inherited acquired characteristics in his Philosophie zoologique of 1809; Erasmus Darwin, Charles's grandfather, had speculated in verse about transmutation in Zoonomia in the 1790s; and the anonymous Vestiges of the Natural History of Creation, published by the Edinburgh publisher Robert Chambers in 1844, had drawn an enormous popular readership and equally enormous scientific hostility for its sketch of a developmental cosmos. What was lacking was a mechanism convincing enough to persuade professional naturalists, and a body of evidence rich enough to overwhelm the prevailing natural theology of William Paley's Watchmaker God, whose Natural Theology of 1802 was still standard reading at Cambridge.

Darwin, born in Shrewsbury in 1809 into a wealthy Whig and Unitarian medical family, had begun as an unlikely candidate to overturn Paley. Sent to Edinburgh to study medicine and then to Christ's College, Cambridge, to train for the Anglican priesthood, he was drawn instead into geology under Adam Sedgwick and into natural history under John Stevens Henslow. It was Henslow who recommended him for the position of gentleman companion to Captain Robert FitzRoy of HMS Beagle, the survey ship that sailed from Devonport on 27 December 1831 for what was intended as a two-year voyage and lasted almost five. The Beagle circumnavigated the globe, surveying the coasts of South America and touching the Galapagos in September and October 1835, Tahiti, New Zealand, Australia, the Cape and Saint Helena on the way home in October 1836.

Back in London and installed by 1842 at Down House in the Kent countryside, Darwin worked systematically through his collections, corresponded with breeders, pigeon fanciers, botanists and zoologists across the empire, and by July 1837 had opened the first of his notebooks on the transmutation of species. Reading Thomas Malthus's Essay on the Principle of Population in September 1838 gave him the mechanism he needed: if more individuals were born than could survive, then whatever heritable variations conferred even a slight advantage would tend to accumulate. He drafted a thirty-five-page pencil sketch in June 1842 and a two hundred and thirty-page essay in 1844, showed it to the botanist Joseph Hooker and later to the geologist Charles Lyell and the biologist Thomas Henry Huxley, but did not publish. For twenty years he polished, waited and worked on other things, including an exhaustive four-volume monograph on barnacles.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The decision to publish was forced on Darwin from the other side of the world. On 18 June 1858 he opened a parcel at Down House and found a twenty-page manuscript from the naturalist Alfred Russel Wallace, then collecting in the Malay Archipelago on the island of Ternate. Wallace, thirty-five years old and financing his fieldwork by selling specimens to collectors, had independently arrived at the same theory of natural selection and asked Darwin to forward the paper to Charles Lyell if he thought it worth publishing. Darwin, appalled and honourable in equal measure, wrote to Lyell that all his originality was to be smashed.

Lyell and Hooker arranged a compromise. On 1 July 1858 at a meeting of the Linnean Society of London in Burlington House, they presented a joint communication under the title 'On the Tendency of Species to form Varieties; and on the Perpetuation of Varieties and Species by Natural Means of Selection'. It consisted of Wallace's Ternate paper, an unpublished sketch of Darwin's from 1844 and a letter Darwin had sent to the American botanist Asa Gray in 1857. Neither Darwin, mourning his infant son who had died of scarlet fever three days earlier, nor Wallace, still in the tropics, was present. The paper attracted almost no attention. The society's president, Thomas Bell, reported at the end of the year that 1858 had produced no revolutionary discoveries.

Darwin spent the next thirteen months writing what he called an abstract of his big species book, working from Down House and from the water-cure spa at Ilkley in Yorkshire. On 28 March 1859 he proposed titles to Murray; on 5 April he sent the first three chapters; through the summer he corrected proofs. Murray printed twelve hundred and fifty copies, of which about a hundred and eighty went to reviewers, presentation and depository copies. On Tuesday 22 November 1859 the trade sale was held at Murray's premises, and Mudie's Circulating Library, then the great arbiter of Victorian reading, took five hundred copies at once. Publication was set for Thursday 24 November at fifteen shillings a copy. The edition sold out. Murray immediately commissioned a second edition of three thousand copies, which appeared on 7 January 1860 with a few softening insertions, including the phrase 'by the Creator' added to the closing sentence.

The book's argument was cumulative. The first four chapters presented variation under domestication, variation in nature, the struggle for existence, and the principle of natural selection. Later chapters dealt with the difficulties of the theory, instinct, hybridism, the imperfection of the geological record, the geographical distribution of species and morphology, embryology and rudimentary organs. Darwin was careful not to claim more than his evidence allowed. He said almost nothing about human evolution, remarking only that 'light will be thrown on the origin of man and his history'. He wrote for the educated general reader as much as for the professional naturalist, a decision that helped make the book one of the most widely discussed scientific works ever published.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The reception was immediate and polarised. The physicist John Herschel, whom Darwin had revered, called natural selection the 'law of higgledy-piggledy'. The comparative anatomist Richard Owen wrote a hostile anonymous review in the Edinburgh Review. But the young Thomas Henry Huxley reviewed the book warmly in the Times of 26 December 1859, wrote to Darwin promising to be his bulldog, and threw himself into the public defence of the theory. Asa Gray in the United States, Ernst Haeckel in Germany, and Fritz Muller in Brazil provided its earliest continental and colonial champions.

The most famous confrontation came on the afternoon of 30 June 1860 at a meeting of the British Association for the Advancement of Science at the Oxford University Museum. Bishop Samuel Wilberforce, primed by Owen, spoke at length against evolution and asked Huxley whether it was through his grandfather or his grandmother that he claimed descent from a monkey. Huxley is said to have replied that he would rather be descended from an ape than from a bishop who used his intellect to obscure the truth. Accounts of what was actually said vary, but the meeting entered the mythology of Victorian science as the moment when the new professional naturalists routed the ecclesiastical establishment.

Within a decade the fact of evolution, if not always the mechanism of natural selection, was accepted by most working biologists. Darwin himself elaborated the theory in a series of books including The Variation of Animals and Plants under Domestication in 1868, The Descent of Man and Selection in Relation to Sex in 1871, in which he finally applied the theory explicitly to humans, and The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals in 1872. Six editions of the Origin appeared during his lifetime, culminating in the sixth of 19 February 1872, from which the title was shortened simply to The Origin of Species and in which the price was reduced to seven shillings and sixpence to widen its readership.

The cultural impact was seismic. The Origin dismantled the biological argument from design. It offered a naturalistic account of the appearance of purpose in living things. It made time, deep geological time, essential to biology. It suggested that humans were part of, not above, the natural order. Combined with the geological revolution of Lyell and later with the physics of Kelvin and the chemistry of Mendeleev, it fed a broader Victorian sense that science was displacing revelation as the source of authoritative knowledge about the natural world. Theologians such as Charles Kingsley and Frederick Temple, who became Archbishop of Canterbury in 1896, argued that the new biology was compatible with Christianity; others rejected the theory outright.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Natural selection took longer than Darwin's other claims to be fully accepted by biologists. Between about 1880 and 1930, in what Julian Huxley later called the eclipse of Darwinism, many biologists preferred alternative mechanisms such as neo-Lamarckism, orthogenesis or mutationism. The problem was that Darwin could not explain how heritable variation was preserved across generations; his own theory of pangenesis was widely rejected. Only when the rediscovery of Gregor Mendel's laws of inheritance after 1900, the population genetics of Ronald Fisher, J. B. S. Haldane and Sewall Wright in the 1920s and 1930s, and the syntheses of Theodosius Dobzhansky, Ernst Mayr and George Gaylord Simpson in the 1930s and 1940s produced the modern evolutionary synthesis, did natural selection resume its place at the centre of the discipline. The discovery of the structure of DNA by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953, and the genetic and genomic revolutions that followed, extended and refined the Darwinian framework rather than replacing it.

The Origin has never been out of print. It has been translated into more than thirty languages, from Bronn's altered German version of 1860 through Clemence Royer's polemical French translation of 1862 to modern editions in Chinese, Arabic, Russian and Japanese. Down House, where Darwin worked for forty years, is now a museum of the National Trust and an English Heritage property. The manuscript of the book itself, preserved at Cambridge University Library, has been digitised and made accessible online along with virtually all of Darwin's forty thousand pages of surviving letters through the Darwin Correspondence Project.

Beyond biology, the theory has been the subject of endless controversy. It has been invoked in support of laissez-faire capitalism, socialist evolutionism, colonialism, anti-colonialism, eugenics, its opposite, and both scientific atheism and modernist Christian theology, often in ways that Darwin himself would not have recognised. The Scopes Trial of 1925 in Tennessee, the 1968 Supreme Court decision in Epperson v. Arkansas, the Kansas evolution hearings of 2005, and the recurring efforts to introduce creationism or intelligent design into public school science curricula testify to the continuing political charge of the ideas that Charles Darwin quietly published in Piccadilly one Thursday morning in November 1859.

worldContext.text: The United States in 1859 was on the brink of civil war. On 16 October the abolitionist John Brown seized the federal arsenal at Harpers Ferry in Virginia with the aim of arming enslaved people; captured by US Marines under Colonel Robert E. Lee, he was hanged on 2 December, three days after the book's publication. The Comstock Lode of silver had been struck in Nevada in June; the Pony Express was in preparation. In Mexico the Reform War between liberals under Benito Juarez and conservatives was in its final phase.

The Qing empire was in the middle of the Second Opium War against Britain and France and simultaneously engulfed by the Taiping Rebellion, which by 1859 had held its capital at Nanjing for six years and controlled much of the Yangzi valley. On 25 June 1859 an Anglo-French naval force was repelled at the Second Battle of Taku Forts; a much larger expedition would take Beijing and burn the Old Summer Palace in October 1860. In Japan, the treaty ports opened by the Harris Treaty of 1858 began operations, admitting foreign residents to Kanagawa, Nagasaki and Hakodate.

The British East India Company had been dissolved by the Government of India Act of 1858 and direct rule from London had begun. Governor-General Lord Canning proclaimed a general amnesty on 8 November 1859, formally ending the great rebellion of 1857 to 1858. The construction of the Indian railway network, which had begun in the 1850s, accelerated; the trunk lines from Bombay and Calcutta into the interior were pressed forward, and the telegraph now connected the presidencies with London via Basra.

In Egypt the excavation of the Suez Canal began at Port Said on 25 April 1859 under the direction of Ferdinand de Lesseps; it would take a decade and much forced labour before its inauguration in November 1869. In the Ottoman Empire the sultan Abdulmecid pursued the Tanzimat reforms in the face of financial pressure from European lenders. The Persian Qajar shah Nasir al-Din presided over a country still recovering from the Anglo-Persian War of 1856 to 1857.

European exploration of the interior accelerated. In 1858 John Hanning Speke had reached Lake Victoria and identified it as the source of the Nile, a claim he defended against Richard Burton in London in 1859. David Livingstone's second Zambezi Expedition of 1858 to 1864 was in its second year. In West Africa, the French governor Louis Faidherbe extended Senegalese territory from Saint-Louis; in southern Africa the Boer republics of the Orange Free State and the South African Republic (Transvaal) consolidated their independence.

In New Zealand tensions between the settler government under Governor Thomas Gore Browne and the Maori Kingitanga (King Movement) that had elected Potatau Te Wherowhero as its first Maori king in 1858 were mounting toward the First Taranaki War of 1860. The Australian colonies were absorbing waves of migrants after the gold rushes of the 1850s; Queensland separated from New South Wales on 6 June 1859 to become its own self-governing colony under Governor Sir George Bowen.

figures.name: Charles Darwin
figures.role: English naturalist who developed the theory of evolution by natural selection and published On the Origin of Species in 1859

figures.name: Alfred Russel Wallace
figures.role: Welsh naturalist who independently arrived at the theory of natural selection while collecting in the Malay Archipelago

figures.name: John Murray
figures.role: London publisher of Byron, Jane Austen, Lyell and Darwin who issued the first edition of Origin at Albemarle Street

figures.name: Joseph Dalton Hooker
figures.role: Director of Kew Gardens, Darwin's closest scientific confidant, and co-organiser of the 1858 Linnean Society paper

figures.name: Charles Lyell
figures.role: Geologist and author of Principles of Geology who prepared the intellectual ground for evolutionary thought

figures.name: Thomas Henry Huxley
figures.role: Comparative anatomist and Darwin's most public defender, celebrated for the Oxford debate with Bishop Wilberforce

images.caption: The title page of the first edition of On the Origin of Species, published by John Murray of Albemarle Street on 24 November 1859.
images.credit: John Murray, London (1859), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Charles Darwin photographed by Julia Margaret Cameron in 1868, nine years after the publication of the Origin.
images.credit: Julia Margaret Cameron (1815 to 1879), via Wikimedia Commons
